Hormuz is reopening, AG crude is flooding back, and the market is extrapolating a supply wave forward. But is the crisis really over?

Inside this episode, @NGCanalyst, @JuneGoh_Sparta, and Abhishek Kumar break down:

▸ Brent in contango – buying opportunity or fair value?
▸ The "Asian crude buffet" – PG glut, Iranian waiver, no China
▸ Refinery runrates: Max is what we need, but can the units deliver?

text: U.S. GAS production, consumption and exports appear to have been balanced for the last two months, stabilising inventories after a steady accumulation earlier in the year. Inventories were 173 billion cubic feet (+7% or +0.47 standard deviations) above the ten-year seasonal average on June 19, essentially unchanged from a surplus of 169 bcf (+9% or +0.41σ) two months earlier. In a sign the market is more balanced, front-month gas futures have risen to almost $3.40 per million British thermal units (the threshold at which gas and coal-fired generation are roughly competitive with each other) from a low of just $2.50 in the middle of April:

Was a pleasure to rejoin @TheStalwart and @tracyalloway to talk about how the world managed to avoid the extraordinarily high demand-destructive pricing I initially thought would have been made necessary by a months-long closure of Hormuz.

Scanning the commodities CTA positioning, and gold and silver are flash-glowing on the radar. That bearish momentum definitely got some extra legs as the week rolled on.

I’ve been totally flat precious metals after Feb, but we might be staring right at a textbook CTA fade out opportunity. 

The structural macro trend and S/D are common knowledge, so if we hold our nerve and build the size with some discipline, this could turn into a beautiful print.

But yeah, the big elephant in the room is King Dollar punching through major overhead resistance and keeping the entire metals complex in a chokehold. 

We’re gonna need the DXY rally to run out of gas before we can pull the trigger.

#gold #silver #copper

This checks out completely. Just looking at the optical satellite prints from 10 hours ago today, loadings are still pacing nowhere near normal levels.

The bottleneck is probably structural—they just don't have the tanker count in place to back up a sustainable production comeback. That said, we've got VLCCs steaming hard from Malacca as we speak, so let's watch the tape and see how fast they can actually ramp up.

Btw Iraq is currently bled dry of cash and starving, so they have to keep pumping out headlines. 

A solid contact of mine confirmed Baghdad was so flat broke they couldn't even cover June public payroll, so they got caught printing 25 trillion dinars just to fund salaries.

The new PM is out here frantically trying to clean up corruption and break up the militias, so he's forced to spam the wires with aggressive rhetoric just to look busy. No need to read too much into it.

#oott #iran

quotedTweet.text:
#Iraq: Basra Oil Company directed operators at West Qurna/2 to suspend pumping entirely and cut production to 50,000 barrels per day effective midnight on June 23, citing the continued absence of available tankers under an ongoing force majeure condition.

According to an internal company document received by Shafaq News, the directive ordered a 100 percent suspension of pumping operations and instructed field operators to store remaining output in on-site tanks. No resumption timeline was specified in the document.

West Qurna/2 is one of Iraq's largest oilfields, located approximately 65 kilometers northwest of Basra in southern Iraq, producing around 460,000 to 480,000 barrels per day and holding an estimated 14 billion barrels of recoverable reserves. The field accounts for nearly 9 percent of Iraq's total crude output.
#oott https://t.co/BbmxLbghWA

text:
#Commodities
The Bloomberg Commodity Index, which tracks a basket of 25 major futures contracts, is heading for a sixth consecutive weekly loss, down 3% this week and 12% since hitting a record high last month. All sectors, except soft commodities - where a 24% weather-driven surge in cocoa has provided support - are trading lower, led by industrial and precious metals as investors adjust to a stronger dollar, a hawkish Federal Reserve, and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. The biggest weekly declines have been recorded in silver (-14%), aluminium (-6%), and crude oil (-6.5%).

Oil resumed its decline after tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz accelerated, following a brief rebound on Thursday when a container ship was struck by an unknown projectile off the coast of Oman. The mini tsunami of released barrels is weighing on the front end of the futures curve, while refined fuel markets remain tight. As a result, refinery margins stay elevated, delaying price relief for end users of diesel, jet fuel, and, not least, gasoline as the Northern Hemisphere driving season reaches its peak.

Gold is trading near USD 4,000 for a third consecutive session and is heading for a fourth weekly loss, with investor sentiment still shaken by the recent selloff as markets adjust to the twin headwinds of a hawkish Fed and a stronger dollar. While the technical breakdown continues to weigh on sentiment, continued declines in energy prices and softer bond yields may eventually reduce pressure on the Federal Reserve to tighten policy further, potentially offering some support to the precious metal.

text:
When the U.S. launched strikes on Iran in late February, the immediate concern was oil prices. The Strait of Hormuz—through which roughly 20% of global oil supply flows—closed, sending energy markets into a spiral and gas prices past $4 a gallon in the U.S. within weeks. Domestically and abroad, people felt the oil supply at the pump: Moody’s estimated it would cost Americans over $100 billion at the pump; gas rationing began in Bangladesh, South Korea imposed a fuel price cap, and so on. That gas scarcity might have sounded like what you’ve heard of the 1970s, but it’s resulted in a historic surge into electric vehicles that is still essentially a trickle.

For the first time in history, more than a quarter of all car sales were EVs, Goldman Sachs commodities team wrote in a recent research report. In the grand scheme, the number is pretty paltry, especially considering the early iteration of the Prius was first released nearly three decades ago, and Teslas and other higher-end EVs first came out nearly 15 years ago. But still, the report finds a 3.4 percentage point increase on global EV sales since the Hormuz shock began, reaching 26.1% of all car sales in May, an all-time high.

The acceleration has been broad-based: 12 of the 15 largest EV markets have seen penetration rise since February, Goldman found. The bank now estimates the shift could shave 0.13 to 0.32 million barrels per day off global oil demand by December 2027, a figure that, while modest against roughly 100 mb/d of total consumption, carries outsized significance for a market already navigating a supply crisis.

“This acceleration in global EV car sales suggests our downside oil price scenario is plausible,” the report read.

Goldman’s analysts project Brent could fall to the mid-$50s per barrel by late 2027 if Hormuz constraints persist and EV momentum holds.

China leads, but the shift is global

No market has moved faster than China, according to the report. EV penetration there has jumped 11.4 percentage points since February, driven by a combination of existing EV infrastructure, competitive domestic pricing, and fuel costs that made gasoline cars increasingly unaffordable as global oil prices spiked. China alone accounts for 61% of the total global increase in EV penetration since February, Goldman found, with OECD markets contributing 21% and non-OECD markets outside China making up the remaining 19%.

The U.S., by contrast, has barely budged: up just 0.1 percentage point, per the report. Goldman attributes that partly to the federal EV tax credit expiring in late 2025, which pulled forward purchases and left demand with less room to grow.

Goldman applies a rule of thumb: every 1 million vehicles that shift from internal combustion to electric cuts road fuel demand by 30,000 barrels per day in the U.S. and 20,000 barrels per day elsewhere. Under the bank’s more conservative “temporary acceleration” scenario—which assumes EV penetration flatlines at its current elevated levels—the cumulative demand hit by the end of 2027 reaches 0.13 mb/d. Under its “persistent acceleration” scenario, where current trends continue through late 2027, the figure rises to 0.32 mb/d.

Goldman cautions those figures likely understate the full picture. The analysis covers only new passenger car sales—it doesn’t capture the behavioral shift Goldman says is already underway within the existing vehicle stock. In China, the bank notes, owners of plug-in hybrids are increasingly opting to charge rather than refuel, contributing to what Goldman estimates as a more than 20% year-over-year drop in gasoline and related product sales volumes. Two- and three-wheeler EVs—which Goldman says command 92% of EV sales in India, 80% in Vietnam, and 35% in China—add further displacement that doesn’t appear in the passenger car data at all.

Reshaping long-term demand

The Goldman report is interesting for what it implies about the relationship between energy shocks and technology adoption. Climate policy, fuel economy standards, and government EV subsidies have spent years nudging consumers toward electric vehicles, but the Iran war may have done more in four months. Goldman’s downside scenario envisions a 1.5 mb/d persistent loss in global oil demand tied to the conflict, versus 0.5 mb/d in its base case—with Brent in the mid-$50s representing a structural headwind for producers betting on sustained demand growth through the decade.

In the U.S., new EV sales in the U.S. totaled roughly 85,000 units in May—just 5.7% of total vehicle sales, down nearly 22% from a year earlier. The collapse traces directly to the expiration of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit in September 2025, which has yet to be replaced.

In China, the dynamic is inverted: EVs accounted for nearly 63% of all retail car sales in May, with gasoline vehicles’ share collapsing to just 37%—and in April, China had already hit a record high of more than 60% electric, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association.

This story was originally featured on https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs

https://t.co/teLInys3gD

Global investment bank Goldman Sachs raises India’s real GDP growth forecast to 6.8% for the calendar year 2026, from its earlier estimate of 6.5%, citing easing oil prices and improving domestic economic conditions. 

In its latest report titled India: Improved Macro Outlook After The US and Iran deal, the global investment bank lowered its headline inflation forecast by 0.2 percentage points to 4.4% and reduced its current account deficit estimate to 1.1% of GDP, amid a sharp correction in global crude oil prices after the US-Iran peace deal. 

#GDP #IndianEconomy #GrowthRate

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 26, 2026

Market Brief

Fifteen-Fifth Five-Year Plan for a New Energy System takes effect; new energy power generation to account for 30% of total by 2030

Traffic volume through the Strait of Hormuz rebounds significantly; Iran asserts sole management of the strait

U.S. May PCE Price Index rose 4.1% year-over-year, the highest in three years

Tech stocks diverged on Thursday; Micron Technology surged over 15%, Apple fell 6%

Chip costs surge; Apple raises prices globally on multiple products

Storage and memory costs more than double; Microsoft announces Xbox console price hikes

IBM unveils world’s first 0.7-nanometer chip technology, breaking physical limits

Top Stories

Apple Inc. announced price increases for Mac, iPad, home devices, and Vision Pro, with adjustments of up to 20% on multiple models to offset sustained surges in memory and storage chip costs. The price hikes are global. Apple did not raise prices for iPhone, Apple Watch, or AirPods. The company stated it has never witnessed such rapid and substantial increases in component prices and had previously absorbed cost pressures for consumers, but now has no choice but to pass them on.

Hours after Apple announced price hikes for MacBook and iPad, Microsoft also issued a statement that consumers will face higher prices for Xbox gaming consoles due to continued increases in key component costs. Microsoft cited storage and memory prices rising over 2.5 times as the primary driver. Starting August 1, prices for multiple Xbox models will increase. Microsoft also announced it will discontinue sales of the 2TB version of the Xbox Series X launched in 2024.

IBM unveiled the world’s first sub-1-nanometer chip technology, employing a revolutionary 3D “nanostack” architecture that pushes the node to 0.7nm. The new chip doubles transistor density, offering a 50% performance boost or 70% energy efficiency gain compared to 2nm nodes, with mass production expected within five years. This marks the semiconductor industry’s formal entry into the atomic-scale era, surpassing previous physical limits.

Oman’s Foreign Minister Badr stated that future arrangements concerning the Strait of Hormuz will not involve any tolls. Iran estimates that charging for security and environmental services related to the strait could generate $40 billion annually for involved nations. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy emphasized that vessels must coordinate passage in advance, and non-compliant ships “will be dealt with.” S&P Global Energy reported that daily traffic through the Strait of Hormuz in June had recovered to approximately 57% of pre-conflict levels.

The Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge was released. Data from the U.S. Department of Commerce showed the U.S. May PCE Price Index rose 4.1% year-over-year, the highest since April 2023; core PCE increased 3.4% year-over-year, matching expectations and marking the highest level since October 2023. Additionally, the final Q1 real GDP growth was revised up to 2.1% annualized, above the prior estimate of 1.6%. May durable goods orders fell 4.5% month-over-month, the largest decline in nearly a year.

China Macro & Policy

The National Development and Reform Commission and the National Energy Administration issued the “Fifteen-Fifth Five-Year Plan for Building a New Energy System,” aiming for a total energy production capacity of 5.8 billion tons of standard coal by 2030, with coal and oil consumption peaking, non-fossil fuel consumption reaching 25%, non-fossil fuel power generation accounting for 50%, and new energy power generation making up 30% of total output.

Six ministries jointly released a five-year plan for “Beautiful China: Public Action,” outlining 15 specific initiatives across five areas. To promote green lifestyle transitions, the plan targets consumption in food, clothing, housing, transportation, daily use, and tourism, exploring incentive mechanisms such as points redemption, commercial discounts, and public service exchanges.

China’s Ministry of Commerce spokesperson He Yadong said that as of June 2026, over 500 key foreign-invested projects have been launched, including more than 70 flagship projects, primarily in chemical energy, electromechanical equipment, electronic information, automotive, and medical health manufacturing, with total investment exceeding $300 billion.

China Equities Markets

A-shares rose amid persistent AI-driven trading, with semiconductor, computing hardware, and CPO concepts surging, pushing the ChiNext Index and Star 50 Index to all-time highs. At close, the SSE Index gained 0.23% to 4,120.28, the Shenzhen Component Index rose 1.82%, the ChiNext Index jumped 2.84%, the Star 50 Index climbed 3.87%, and the Wind All China Index advanced 0.82%. Market turnover reached 3.62 trillion yuan. Brokerage stocks rose again, while precious metals, oil and gas, chemicals, coal, and non-ferrous metals lagged.

Hong Kong markets declined, with the Hang Seng Index falling 1.43% to 23,076.91 and the Hang Seng Tech Index dropping 1.63%, both hitting their lowest levels in over a year. The Hang Seng China Enterprises Index slid 2.02%. Non-ferrous metals, consumer, and coal stocks underperformed, while semiconductors and CPO concepts surged. Northbound funds sold a net 8.87 billion Hong Kong dollars, with Alibaba hit by a net outflow of 2.25 billion Hong Kong dollars, while Jian Tong Group and SMIC each saw net inflows exceeding 2.6 billion Hong Kong dollars.

The 2026 interim report schedule for Shanghai-listed companies was released. Zhongchuan Special Gases will be first to report on July 18. Haitong Development will report on July 21, while Longruan Technology and Shentong Technology will release results on July 23.

Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing announced it will optimize strike price intervals for weekly stock and index options to enhance market liquidity. The changes, pending regulatory approval, will take effect on July 27. HKEX also plans to launch T+1 session optimizations on July 20.

China Industry News

Five ministries jointly issued the “Action Plan for Innovative Applications of AI in Transportation,” focusing on pilot demonstrations in autonomous driving, smart highways, intelligent railways, smart shipping, intelligent aviation, smart postal services, smart construction and maintenance, intelligent travel services, smart freight, and intelligent safety supervision.

The global technical regulation for autonomous driving systems (ADS GTR), jointly led by China, the EU, the UK, the U.S., Canada, and Japan, was formally approved and released after voting by all contracting parties.

The State Administration for Market Regulation held its second 2026 corporate fair competition symposium, with representatives from manufacturing, AI, pharmaceuticals, and construction industries discussing “removing bottlenecks to uphold fair market competition.”

The National Energy Administration reported that as of end-May, China’s total installed power generation capacity reached 4.01 billion kilowatts, up 11% year-over-year and the highest globally. Solar capacity hit 1.26 billion kilowatts (+16.3%), and wind capacity reached 660 million kilowatts (+17%).

The National Radio and Television Administration released a classification and tiered management standard for AI-generated short videos, categorizing them as key, ordinary, or other based on investment and subject matter. Projects investing 800,000 yuan or more, or those involving national security themes, are classified as “key short videos.”

A prime residential land parcel in Bao’an District, Shenzhen, was auctioned with an opening price of 5.28 billion yuan. After over 300 rounds of bidding, Poly secured the land for a total of 10.51 billion yuan, a premium of over 99%, with a floor price of 87,900 yuan per square meter, ranking among the top three in Shenzhen’s residential land price history.

Fintech

U.S. Democratic members of the House of Representatives urged the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to respond to regulatory questions regarding AI trading agents by July 31. These tools, currently operating outside existing securities regulations, can make major investment decisions on behalf of retail investors and are expanding from cryptocurrency into options and futures markets.

Corporate News & Updates

Sources say Apple is making its largest-ever strategic adjustment to its Mac chip development, planning to launch a new AI-focused processor line for next-generation high-end chips. Insiders revealed Apple’s current chip iteration is the M5 series, with a basic M6 processor expected as early as this year for entry-level Mac devices.

DeepSeek posted job ads stating that as technology evolves, it aims to expand the size of all departments by at least 100%. Open positions include server-side development engineers, pre-training data engineers, AI search algorithm/architecture engineers, Agent Harness team, Agent Infra engineers, front-end/client-side developers, and AI cross-disciplinary talent.

Hurun Research Institute’s “2026 Global Unicorn Index” showed global unicorn companies reached a record high of 1,603, up 80 from last year. Anthropic, OpenAI, and ByteDance ranked first, second, and third with valuations of 6.6 trillion yuan, 5.8 trillion yuan, and 3.3 trillion yuan, respectively. DeepSeek ranked in the top 15 with a valuation of 340 billion yuan.

TSMC forecasted that global semiconductor market revenue will surpass $1 trillion this year and reach $1.5 trillion by 2030. TSMC expects demand to be driven primarily by high-performance computing and AI, accounting for 55% of total market; followed by smartphones (20%), automobiles (10%), and IoT (10%).

Huawei’s Harmony Intelligent Mobility unveiled the Zunjie S800 Grand Design Collector’s Edition, with a pre-sale price range of 1.6 to 2 million yuan, targeting the ultra-luxury vehicle market above 2 million yuan. Sales data show the current Zunjie S800 has delivered 19,000 units and has topped the million-yuan luxury vehicle sales chart for nine consecutive months.

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 7-2 that consumers cannot sue Bayer over its Roundup herbicide for failing to warn of cancer risks. The ruling is expected to end nearly a decade of litigation that has cost Bayer over $10 billion.

Amazon announced it will increase its investment in India by $13 billion to expand AI and cloud infrastructure, boosting AWS data center capacity in Mumbai and Hyderabad.

Global Macro & Policy

The U.S. Supreme Court issued two key rulings supporting Trump administration immigration policies. One allowed the administration to rescind temporary protected status for hundreds of thousands of immigrants. Another permitted enforcement of policies blocking asylum seekers from entering U.S. territory.

New York Fed President John Williams said interest rates are at a level conducive to bringing inflation back toward the central bank’s target. He expects inflation to ease to 3.5% by year-end, then gradually decline to 2% by 2028.

The U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) is updating regulations to eliminate the requirement that autonomous vehicles must be equipped with brake pedals, lowering regulatory barriers for Tesla, Waymo, and others seeking to expand autonomous taxi deployments in the U.S. Other vehicles will still require brake pedals.

The U.S. Department of Labor reported that initial jobless claims fell by 12,000 to 215,000 for the week ending June 20, below the forecast of 225,000. Continuing claims rose slightly to 1.821 million, above the expected 1.8 million.

Haruhiko Tamura, a hawkish member of the Bank of Japan’s policy board, explicitly called for interest rate hikes every few months, aiming to gradually raise policy rates toward the estimated 2% neutral rate. He added that if inflation risks intensify, the central bank “must not hesitate to accelerate the pace of hikes.”

The European Council formally approved implementation of tariff commitments under the EU-U.S. Joint Statement, endorsing the trade agreement before the deadline set by U.S. President Trump.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed mixed: the Dow Jones rose 0.14% to 51,920.62, the S&P 500 edged down 0.01% to 7,357.49, and the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.46% to 25,358.60. Apple shares dropped 6.12%, its steepest decline since April 2025. Storage chip stocks surged: SanDisk rose nearly 22%, Micron Technology gained over 15%, Applied Materials climbed over 13%, Western Digital rose nearly 5%, ASML advanced over 4%, and Qualcomm rose over 3%. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index declined 2.75%, with Microsoft and Amazon each falling over 3%, and Meta down over 2%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index dropped 2.7%, with Dindong Buycai falling over 8%.

European indices closed higher: Germany’s DAX rose 1.03% to 24,994.83, France’s CAC 40 gained 0.55% to 8,431.61, and the UK’s FTSE 100 climbed 0.65% to 10,529.89. Eurozone economic data exceeded expectations: manufacturing PMI rose to 47.8 in June, while services PMI held steady at 52.3, signaling easing recession risks and improving market sentiment.

AI-driven trading revived, sending Japanese and South Korean markets sharply higher. Japan’s Nikkei 225 surged 4.61% to 72,366.34, a new closing record. South Korea’s KOSPI rose 5.42% to 8,930.30, triggering a circuit breaker during trading. AI stocks soared: SK Hynix jumped 13%, Kioxia gained over 12%, SoftBank rose nearly 8%, and Samsung Electronics climbed over 5%. India’s S&P BSE Sensex edged up 0.14% to 77,100.47.

ETF independent research firm ETFGI reported that as of end-May, global actively managed ETF assets reached a record $2.49 trillion. In May, global active ETFs attracted net inflows of $100.08 billion, bringing year-to-date inflows to $411.75 billion, far exceeding the $220.53 billion for all of 2025.

Sources indicate OpenAI prefers to delay its IPO until next year.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market showed renewed strength, with major government bond yields generally declining, especially for one-year and seven-year tenors. Treasury futures mostly rose, with the 30-year contract leading gains at 0.33%. The People’s Bank of China net injected 122.5 billion yuan via seven-day reverse repos, coupled with a 200-billion-yuan rollover of MLF, improving liquidity conditions. The PBOC also announced it will add overnight reverse repo operations on June 29 and June 30.

U.S. Treasury yields moved mixed: the two-year yield fell 2.05 basis points to 4.121%, the three-year yield dropped 1.41 basis points to 4.125%, the five-year yield declined 0.52 basis points to 4.165%, the 10-year yield rose 0.20 basis points to 4.392%, and the 30-year yield climbed 2.05 basis points to 4.861%.

Commodities

U.S. crude oil futures rose 1.61% to $71.47 per barrel; Brent crude climbed 1.65% to $75.09 per barrel. The rally followed a sharp prior decline and was supported by unexpected U.S. crude inventory draws.

Precious metals closed mixed: COMEX gold futures gained 0.82% to $4,041.60 per ounce; COMEX silver futures fell 0.34% to $57.89 per ounce. Fed’s persistent inflation control with delayed target timing, IMF policy endorsement, and weakening geopolitical risk sentiment, combined with record-low domestic inventory, drove divergent price movements.

Most base metals on the LME rose: aluminum gained 2.02% to $3,185.50 per tonne, copper advanced 1.75% to $13,316.00 per tonne, tin rose 0.94% to $50,150.00 per tonne, zinc climbed 0.48% to $3,438.00 per tonne, nickel edged up 0.28% to $16,865.00 per tonne, and lead dipped 0.03% to $1,912.50 per tonne.

As June 30 approaches, global AI computing metals face new volatility. The U.S. Section 232 tariff review will conclude, and expectations of new copper tariffs have already disrupted markets. If implemented, Goldman Sachs and other institutions expect a fresh U.S. stockpiling surge. COMEX inventories have climbed above 650,000 tonnes, a historical high. Tin, tantalum, and indium—closely tied to AI computing—may see further price catalysts.

An Iraqi oil ministry official told media that if Iraq’s oil production quota is not significantly increased, the government is “considering all feasible options, including exiting OPEC.” The ministry later clarified that the government has never proposed exiting OPEC and that the report does not reflect official policy.

China Construction Bank announced it will shut down its agency individual precious metals trading services with the Shanghai Gold Exchange effective at the end of day on July 24.

FX & Currency Markets

On Thursday, the onshore yuan closed at 6.7995 per U.S. dollar, up 57 basis points from the previous close; the offshore yuan closed at 6.7990. The PBOC’s daily reference rate was set at 6.8209, a 14-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York close, the U.S. dollar index fell 0.12% to 101.46. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro gained 0.10% to 1.1370 against the dollar, the pound rose 0.18% to 1.3192, the Australian dollar climbed 0.15% to 0.6911, the dollar-yen held flat at 161.8045, the dollar-Canadian dollar fell 0.23% to 1.4200, the dollar-franc dropped 0.26% to 0.8102, and the offshore yuan rose 116 basis points to 6.8016 against the dollar.

Key Economic Events

04:00 EDT, Jun 26: ECB 1-year/3-year CPI expectations for May in Europe

05:00 EDT, Jun 26: Italy Istat Consumer Confidence Index for June

08:30 EDT, Jun 26: U.S. Wholesale Inventories for May

10:00 EDT, Jun 26: U.S. University of Michigan Final Consumer Confidence Index for June

10:00 EDT, Jun 26: U.S. Final 1-year Inflation Expectations for June

01:00 EDT, Jun 27: U.S. Weekly Oil Drilling Rig Count through Jun 26

22:00 EDT, Jun 25: State Council Information Office holds press conference on plans for accelerating the construction of a new energy system during the 15th Five-Year Plan period

03:00 EDT, Jun 26: National Health Commission holds press conference on promoting reproductive health

09:45 EDT, Jun 26: ECB Executive Board member Schnabel speaks

11:30 EDT, Jun 26: 2026 FOMC voter and Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari speaks

24:00 EDT, Jun 26: ECB Deputy Governor Vujčić speaks

2026 China (Shenzhen) Integrated Circuit Summit convened

2026 Beijing International Low-altitude Economy and UAV Systems Industry Exhibition to be held from Jun 26 to 28

New Zealand Reserve Bank Governor Breman attends the 25th Annual Meeting of the Bank for International Settlements (BIS)

[New Listings] Lingyi Smart Manufacturing H-shares, Ketao Shares H-shares, Shengbang Shares H-shares, Xincheng Microequipment H-shares, Zhongke Wenge, MERDEKAGOLD-DRS listed on HKEX; Huake Shares listed on SZSE
https://t.co/GNAypjWyBH
https://t.co/iODvgIWm99

GOLDMAN SACS RAISED INDIA CY26 GDP FORECAST TO 6.8% FROM 6.5% 

"Overall, Q2 CY26 real GDP growth is tracking above our earlier expectations," Goldman Sachs said, adding that the stronger growth trajectory, coupled with lower oil price assumptions, prompted an upward revision.

GOLDMAN SACS RAISED INDIA CY26 GDP FORECAST TO 6.8 % FROM 6.5 % 

"Overall, Q2 CY26 real GDP growth is tracking above our earlier expectations," Goldman Sachs said, adding that the stronger growth trajectory, coupled with lower oil price assumptions, prompted an upward revision.

MACRO:

OpenAI is considering holding off on its IPO until next year, according to NYT sources. The report noted there was concern over the decline in SpaceX shares post IPO and Altman did not want to compromise on his $1trln market cap valuation. (NYT)

The Trump administration has asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its state of the art AI model so that organisations set to gain early access to the powerful tool can first be vetted by the US government. Meanwhile, negotiations between Anthropic and the US over reinstating access to Mythos are ongoing. (FT)

South Korean stocks were hit by a second trading suspension this week as chipmakers plunged. Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix each fell more than 10% at one point. (BBG)

Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are preparing to announce new investments worth hundreds of billions of dollars on Monday, according to South Korean media reports. Samsung is set to unveil a 1,000 trillion won ($646 billion) spending package over the next decade. Samsung's investment will include spending on AI data centres, batteries and displays as well as a potential 300 trillion won push to build chip factories in the country's southwest. (BBG/RTRS)

BofA's flow show notes tech stock funds had record $9.3b outflows, just one week after posting record $19.2b inflows, in a week where investors exited cash and bought bonds. US stocks had their first outflow since March at $8.5b following record $119.2b inflow the previous week. Treasuries had 1st outflow in 9 weeks at $94m. (BBG)

Kevin Warsh’s vow to rein in inflation has bolstered the new Federal Reserve chair’s credibility in financial markets, big fund managers say, helping to drive down investors’ expectations for long-term price rises. (FT)

Citi strategists say “It is premature to call for the end of the AI trade”, noting equities are likely to keep churning higher as corporate earnings remain strong, while “liquidity has not tightened enough to burst the bubble”. (BBG)

'Hegseth Brushed Off Congress for Months. Now He Needs Them for His Budget' WSJ article notes the Defense Secretary is struggling to secure Republican support for President Trump’s proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget, which includes using the budget reconciliation process. (WSJ)

A New York City panel approved a rent freeze for roughly one million rent-regulated apartments in a win for Mayor Zohran Mamdani, whose promise to cap housing costs was a signature part of his election campaign. (WSJ)

The White House is poised to nominate Adam Candeub, the general counsel at the FCC, to take over as the Justice Department’s antitrust chief, according to Bloomberg sources. (BBG)

WSJ reports Stanley Woodward, a top US Justice Department official, said he prefers that antitrust enforcers settle cases instead of taking them to trial. Woodward’s stance could weaken the department’s ability to challenge anticompetitive mergers and speed up settlements. (WSJ)

Annual core inflation in Japan's capital accelerated in June, a sign of broadening price pressures from the Middle East conflict that keeps the central bank on track to consider further raise interest rates. (RTRS)

The Japanese government will continue to maintain fiscal sustainability as it compiles budgets and makes investment decisions, the finance minister said, after PM Takaichi unveiled a $2.3 trillion, long-term investment roadmap along with sweeping budget reforms. (BBG)

The yen’s weakness is seen capped at 162 per dollar because of concerns that Japanese authorities could intervene in the currency market, Michiyoshi Kato, a senior adviser at Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Bank said. Notes a sizable amount of FX options is clustered around the 162 level. (BBG)

China's central bank instructed some commercial banks to increase their lending this month, according to Reuters sources, the latest sign that demand for credit remains weak as the economy grapples with sluggish domestic consumption. (RTRS)

China is preparing new legal tools for striking back against economic pressure from abroad, raising risks for foreign businesses operating in the world’s second-largest economy. The proposed law, currently in its second reading, would add to China’s existing legal mechanisms countering foreign sanctions and coercion. (WSJ)

China's central bank is expected to price its inaugural overnight reverse repo operation slightly below the seven-day rate when it debuts the tool on Monday. The Bloomberg median estimate of 17 analysts and economists is a rate of 1.35% for the overnight cash injection, which compares with the 1.4% rate on its standard seven-day reverse repo. (BBG)

China is set to import even fewer barrels of oil this month, extending its exceptionally subdued appetite since the start of the Iran war. (BBG)

China press says the country can create more job opportunities for young people by tapping into growth industries driven by new productive forces, as rapid expansion in sectors such as artificial intelligence, robotics and semiconductors are generating strong demand for skilled workers. (Economic Daily)

T. Rowe Price is betting on a pullback in the yuan, arguing that it looks “expensive” against a basket of currencies from China’s major trading partners. (BBG)

Private home prices in Hong Kong rose 1.4% in May from a month earlier, marking a 12th consecutive increase, data from the Rating and Valuation Department showed on Friday, lifted by upbeat market sentiment and demand from mainland Chinese buyers. (RTRS)

Inflation expectations among UK households followed oil prices lower in June, the latest sign the Bank of England may be able to avoid hiking interest rates. (BBG)

ECB's latest consumer survey showed inflation expectations among euro-area consumers for the next year fell significantly in May, even before a deal was reached to end the hostilities in the Middle East. (BBG)

The ECB will retire a number of recommendations and guides for banks and soften its expectations about good governance, it said on Friday, partly in response to industry criticism. (RTRS)

French Finance Minister Roland Lescure reaffirmed his commitment to lower France’s deficit after the country’s fiscal watchdog rebuked the government’s pending plans. “I want us to do everything we can to maintain the 5% target,” Lescure said Friday on France Culture radio. (BBG)

Thailand's central bank chief said on Friday it will not raise interest rates if it is not necessary to do so. There is no need to raise rates for now, Bank of Thailand Governor Vitai Ratanakorn told reporters. (RTRS)

IRAN:

Iran’s IRGC attacked a Singapore-flagged cargo ship Thursday in the Strait of Hormuz, according to two senior U.S. officials cited by the WSJ, testing the deal signed last week by the U.S. and Iran to end the fighting and reopen the vital shipping lane. The incident took place near the coast of Oman hours after the Iranian paramilitary’s navy warned ships not to use routes through the waterway that the regime hadn’t sanctioned. (WSJ)

Saudi Aramco resumed crude loadings on Friday at its Ras Tanura terminal in the Gulf after a near four-month halt, shipping data showed, as the world's biggest oil exporter joined a rush to move cargoes amid industry hopes of a return to normal. (RTRS)

QatarEnergy has issued a tender to sell crude for July-to-August loading, likely its first since the U.S.-Iran conflict began, trade sources said to Reuters on Friday. (RTRS)

Empty liquefied natural gas tankers are lining up outside of Qatar’s massive export plant in the Persian Gulf as the supplier seeks to quickly increase production following early progress in US-Iran peace talks. (BBG)

The interim U.S.-Iran peace accord gives U.N. nuclear inspectors access to Iran, the watchdog's top official said on Friday, after Tehran indicated key sites would remain off-limits until a final deal with Washington was reached and sanctions lifted. (RTRS)

GEOPOLITICAL:

Ukraine’s escalating long-range drone and cruise missile campaign has damaged 20% of Russia’s refining capacity since March. Russia’s vast territory and air defense systems, designed for crewed aircraft, struggle against Ukraine’s drone swarms. (WSJ)

Kazakhstan’s giant Karachaganak oil and gas field cut crude production by more than a quarter after a drone attack forced the shutdown of a processing plant in Russia that handles its gas. (BBG)

Canada and Japan are working on a range of mining projects, including potential joint stockpiling, Canada's trade minister told Reuters, as Japan moves to diversify supplies of critical minerals and reduce dependence on China. (RTRS)

EQUITIES:

ON Semiconductor agreed to acquire Synaptics in an all-stock transaction with an enterprise value of around $7 billion. ON Semi said Synaptics’s AI compute platform, human-machine interface technology, and connectivity solutions would help it meet demand for increasingly capable AI solutions that can interact with the physical world. (WSJ)

'Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle' NYT article notes Apple has agreed to begin manufacturing a small portion of its laptop chips at Intel’s factories as early as 2027, according to sources, which note some smartphone chips may follow. (NYT)

Amazon Web Services (AWS) will raise prices on its GPU cloud instances by 20% starting July 1, 2026 — including older NVIDIA A100 chips widely used for AI training. (AMZN)

Korea media suggests SK hynix’s inclusion in the Nasdaq 100 within this year is considered a near certainty. They also reported that the possibility of Samsung Electronics pursuing an ADR is being discussed in Korea’s securities industry. (MK via @jukan05)

SpaceX has told investors it plans to launch a Starlink mobile service for U.S. consumers, accoridng to the FT, in a move that could allow the Elon Musk-led company to compete directly with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile. (FT)

'The Options Trader Who Emerged as a Surprise Front-Runner to Succeed Jamie Dimon' WSJ article profiles Troy Rohrbaugh, who is seen as the leading contender in a race with Doug Petno to run JPMorgan. (WSJ)

FedEx Freight Holding Co. is pursuing new business in sectors including data center infrastructure, grocery and healthcare to chart its future as a newly independent company. (BBG)

Donald Trump Jr was handed about $300,000 worth of equity in Kalshi after joining the prediction market platform as a strategic adviser in early 2025, according to FT sources, at a valuation of $2bln, less than a tenth of its $22bn valuation in a funding round last month. FT reports Kalshi has benefited from a light-touch regulatory environment under the Trump administration. (FT)

Binance has told its customers in the EU that it will stop providing services to them from next week because it will not be licensed to operate in the region, in a significant setback for the world’s biggest crypto exchange. (FT)

OpenAI’s new Jalapeno AI inference chip will be made by TSMC on 3nm, while the Jalapeno2 will use A16 and CoWoS advanced packaging, media report. (UDN via @dnystedt)

Volkswagen CEO Oliver Blume aims to slash up to 100,000 jobs from its current workforce worldwide over the next few years, Manager Magazin reported on Friday. (RTRS)

Shares in Zalando fell after Germany’s financial regulator said it launched an audit of the company’s financial statements, alleging that a transaction related to its acquisition of About You might have been omitted. (WSJ)

Equinor has decided to end its offshore wind business activities in Japan and close its Tokyo office by the end of 2026, the company said on its website. (RTRS)

Deeper dive into data: In other data this morning, the third and last government estimate for first quarter gross domestic product (GDP) surprisingly jumped to 2.1% from 1.6%. Analysts had expected no change. In less positive news, the government revised first quarter personal consumption in the GDP report down to 0.5% from the initial 1.4% estimate. The 0.5% increase was its smallest annualized quarterly increase since the first quarter of 2022. "The upward revision to overall GDP should come with an asterisk since the consumer tends to be a big driver of the economy, and it's not good news that personal consumption was lower than initially expected," Martin said. Additionally, the GDP deflator, which measures prices across the economy, rose 3.6% last quarter, above the 3.5% second estimate. Weekly initial jobless claims fell to 215,000, below the 225,000 consensus estimate from https://t.co/dZVXpz58Uf. May personal spending and personal income both climbed 0.7%, but personal spending in April, was revised down to 0.4% from 0.5% in April. Treasury note yields, already at one-month lows, extended their decline after the data.

Micron earnings and early chip action: Micron's earnings appeared to make a good impression as shares of the $1 trillion memory chip firm surged 18% ahead of the open. Its strength translated into positive performance for other chip stocks after two sessions of struggles. Checking the data, Micron's earnings per share of $25.11 easily beat consensus of $20.83 while revenue of $41.46 billion topped consensus of $35.85 billion. Gross margin of 84.9% was up sequentially, and the company cited "rapidly growing demand." For the current quarter, Micron projected earnings per share between $30 and $32, well above consensus of $25.72, and revenue of $49 billion to $51 billion. That topped consensus of $43.58 billion, per FactSet. Though Micron's results revived the chip sector this morning after the "great chip dip" earlier this week, one solid earnings report doesn't necessarily negate factors that drove the recent unwinding of crowded positioning. Concerns remain about returns on massive AI investments, concentration of investor money in chip, hyperscaler, and AI-related names, and the possible slowing of compute demand, said Liz Ann Sonders, chief investment strategist at SCFR.

Fed rate hike not certain: The U.S. dollar index hit new 2026 highs Wednesday, supported by ideas the Federal Reserve could hike rates at least once this year. It's fully priced in by October, and there's a rising implied volatility that one could come in September. That's far from assured. "We are not there yet—we need to see how the inflation outlook evolves over the next few months before we become more certain a hike is likely," Martin said. Last week's Fed statement made it clear that the committee favors its inflation mandate over its labor market mandate right now given recent strength in labor. Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh repeatedly stated that the committee was "unambiguous and unanimous" in its commitment to bringing inflation down. The updated "dot plot" of rate expectations also showed that nine participants now project a hike this year. That' doesn't really match the comments from Fed officials over the last month or so where there didn't seem to be an urgency to hike. Inflation trends may become more favorable with the recent drop in oil. In another development, the Fed's annual bank stress test confirmed banks are well positioned to weather a severe recession and continue lending to households and businesses. Banks often announce dividend increases after passing this test.

text: Oil heads for another losing week, but analyst warns sluggish Strait of Hormuz traffic could send price soaring again https://t.co/Or75IaYYSF #news

The UN has paused its maritime evacuation operation in the Strait of Hormuz following a reported attack on a cargo vessel near Oman. The ship was allegedly targeted by a drone, raising immediate concerns over the stability of the fragile agreement aimed at ending the ongoing war with Iran.

Tensions remain high as Iran continues to assert control over the waterway, warning that ships must follow designated routes or face consequences. Despite efforts to clear the area of stranded vessels and seafarers, the incident has caused oil prices to rise and cast doubt on the future security of this critical global shipping chokepoint. While diplomatic talks are underway, the situation remains fluid and continues to impact energy markets and international stability.

#Hormuz #MiddleEast #Shipping #GlobalEconomy #GlobalArabNetwork

@FoxNews "One question: Before the war with Iran, when the Strait of Hormuz was open, you went in and fought for 40 days but couldn’t even handle 4 mullahs — you ran away. Now you’re proudly paying tribute to the mullah so he allows oil to pass through. Who’s the crazy one here?"🙄😉

$37M is now betting on when the world's most important oil chokepoint reopens.

The Strait of Hormuz moves ~20% of the world's oil. One narrow passage.

It's been near-paralyzed since February. ~100 ships a day → a handful.

Polymarket's read on "back to normal":

By June 30 — 12¢. Basically gone.
By July 7 — 33¢.
By July 15 — 45¢.
By July 31 — 59¢.

A US-Iran deal in mid-June moved a few stranded tankers.

But Kpler shows one-time exits, not a real reopening.

Mines, war-risk insurance, IRGC sign-offs — still in the way.

The market isn't pricing peace. It's pricing how long the bleed lasts.

Which date are you taking?

What we can know without even being privy to high level intel briefs only the Chief has access to is that the Middle East connects directly to our many partnerships in East Asia and Europe. Both of these regions are heavily dependent on energy flow out of the Middle East. 

An Iran that is under the thumb of the Mullahs and the IRGC and also pursuing a nuclear weapon is absolutely a threat to the United States thereby. How do you think we’d be doing right now if a nuclear armed Iran decided it was in its best interests to close the strait? We’d be in a lot more pain at the pump then. They’d be in the position to start imposing their demands on us or threaten nuclear fire if we try to break open the strait. This is a state that funds terrorist orgs all over the Middle East. They’re not above doing something like this. 

Yes, we should be absolutely doing everything we can to reduce the dependence of these regions on the Middle East for oil. If you want this, keep voting Republican because Democrats (probably on Chinese and OPEC payroll) have been obstructing this national security necessity for decades.
